human_truth: The system is trying to log in with the wrong username — it's using your Mac's username instead of your server username.
fix_steps: Check what username your server expects (e.g., 'ubuntu' or 'phoenix')
Add 'ansible_user=YOUR_SERVER_USERNAME' to your inventory file next to the IP address
Or use the -u flag when running ansible: ansible-playbook playbook.yml -u ubuntu

human_truth: Your inventory file has the IP address in the wrong place — it needs to come before the username setting, not after.
fix_steps: Open your inventory file
Find the line causing the error
Make sure it follows this format: 192.168.1.1 ansible_user=ubuntu
The IP address always comes first, then the settings after it

human_truth: Your playbook is trying to run as admin (become: yes) but doesn't have the password to do that.
fix_steps: Add the -K flag when running your playbook: ansible-playbook playbook.yml -K
It will then ask you for your sudo password once
Or for a permanent fix: add your user to sudoers with NOPASSWD (ask for help with this step)

robot_error: Colons in unquoted values must be followed by a non-space character
human_truth: There's a hidden extra space after a colon in your YAML file — YAML is very fussy about spaces.
fix_steps: Look at the line and column number in the error (e.g., line 8, column 10)
Go to that exact line in your file
Look for a colon followed by a space where there shouldn't be one
Remove the extra space — or wrap the value in quotes if it needs a colon in it

human_truth: Something in your YAML file is indented at the wrong level — it's like a bullet point that's been pushed too far to the right.
fix_steps: Find the line number mentioned in the error
Check that all your tasks (lines starting with - name:) are at the same indentation level
Use spaces not tabs — YAML does not accept tab characters
Each level of indentation should be exactly 2 spaces

human_truth: You used a word from Docker Compose (environment:) inside an Ansible task — Ansible uses a different word for the same thing.
fix_steps: Find 'environment:' in your Ansible task
Change it to 'env:' instead
These two words mean the same thing but Ansible only accepts 'env:'

human_truth: Your disk might be completely full — when that happens, apps crash with vague errors instead of telling you the real problem.
fix_steps: Run: df -h
Look for any line showing 100% or close to it
If your disk is full: run 'docker system prune -f' to remove unused Docker files
If that doesn't help, you may need to expand your disk or delete old files

human_truth: This is asking if you want to add a password to your SSH key. For automation to work, you must leave this blank.
fix_steps: ⚠️  STOP — do not type a password here
Just press ENTER to leave it blank
Press ENTER again to confirm
If you already added a passphrase, you will need to regenerate your key without one

human_truth: Your VM was given more disk space but Ubuntu hasn't been told to use it yet. The space exists but is locked away.
fix_steps: Run: df -h to check current disk usage
Run: lsblk to see all available disk space
Run: sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/ubuntu-vg/ubuntu-lv
Then run: sudo resize2fs /dev/ubuntu-vg/ubuntu-lv
Run df -h again to confirm the space is now available

human_truth: The server you are trying to reach is not listening for connections — either the service isn't running, or you have the wrong port number.
fix_steps: Check the service is actually running: sudo systemctl status SERVICE_NAME
Check you have the right port number
Check your firewall isn't blocking the connection: sudo ufw status
Try restarting the service: sudo systemctl restart SERVICE_NAME

human_truth: Your computer can't find the address you gave it — either the hostname doesn't exist, or DNS isn't working.
fix_steps: Check you haven't made a typo in the hostname
Try using the IP address instead of the hostname to test
Check your DNS is working: ping 8.8.8.8
If using a custom hostname like 'ansible-lab.orb.local', make sure OrbStack is running

human_truth: Git can't find the repository you're pointing at — either the URL is wrong, the repo doesn't exist yet, or you don't have permission to access it.
fix_steps: Check the URL is correct — look for typos in the username or repo name
Make sure the repository actually exists — log into Gitea or GitHub and check
Check you're using the right username — run: git remote -v to see the current URL
If the URL is wrong, fix it with: git remote set-url origin YOUR_CORRECT_URL
If the repo doesn't exist yet, create it first then push

human_truth: Git tried to log in but the username or password was wrong — or your Git config is using a different username than your account.
fix_steps: Check your Git config username: git config --global user.name
Update it if wrong: git config --global user.name 'YOUR_USERNAME'
Update your email too: git config --global user.email 'YOUR_EMAIL'
Try pushing again and enter your correct password when prompted
If it still fails, check the remote URL matches your actual username: git remote -v

robot_error: src refspec main does not match any
human_truth: Git is trying to push a branch called 'main' but it doesn't exist yet — you need to make your first commit before you can push.
fix_steps: Make sure you have at least one commit: git add -A && git commit -m 'first commit'
Then push with: git push origin main
If your branch is called 'master' instead of 'main', use: git push origin master

human_truth: Someone else (or another machine) pushed changes to the repo while you were working. Your copy is behind — you need to grab those changes before you can push yours.
fix_steps: Run: git pull --rebase origin main
This grabs the latest changes and puts your work on top
Then push again: git push origin main
If you're using GitSpeak, the 'save' command now handles this automatically

robot_error: fatal: Need to specify how to reconcile divergent branches
human_truth: Your local copy and the remote copy have both changed independently — Git doesn't know which version to trust and needs you to decide.
fix_steps: Run: git pull --rebase origin main
Rebase keeps your history clean and is the recommended option
If GitSpeak's pull command is failing, this is why — update GitSpeak to the latest version
If you get conflicts after rebasing, open the files marked as conflicted and choose which changes to keep

robot_error: nothing to commit, working tree clean
human_truth: There's nothing new to save — all your files are already committed. This isn't an error, it just means everything is up to date locally.
fix_steps: This is not actually an error — your files are already saved
If you're trying to push to remote, run: git push origin main
If you expected changes to be here, check you saved your files in your editor first
Run: git status to see exactly what state everything is in

robot_error: Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
human_truth: The connection to your Git server was suddenly cut off mid-transfer — usually because the server went down or restarted while you were pushing.
fix_steps: Check your Gitea server is still running: docker ps | grep gitea
If OrbStack restarted, wait 30 seconds and try again
Try pushing again: git push origin main
If it keeps happening, check if OrbStack or Docker has crashed
